title: Vermont DOC Prison-Month Level Data Request

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Vermont Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

This is a request under the Vermont Public Records Law. I would like to request both that a document containing facility-month level data from January 2024 through December 2025 be provided on the below measures for every facility managed or owned by Vermont Department of Corrections. Please provide this information in a spreadsheet, if possible: 
* Overall staff budgeted positions (per prison, per month)
* Overall staff filled positions (per prison, per month)
* Custody staff budgeted positions (per prison, per month)
* Custody staff filled positions (per prison, per month)
* Average hours of overall staff overtime (per prison, per month)
* Average hours of custody staff overtime (per prison, per month)
* Average hours of overall staff sick leave (per prison, per month)
* Average hours of custody staff sick leave (per prison, per month)
* Overall population count (per prison, per month)
* Total number of infractions (per prison, per month)
* Total number of violent infractions (including the department’s definition of which infractions are defined as violent) (per prison, per month)
* Total number in restricted housing (per prison, per month)
* Total number of appeals from incarcerated individuals (per prison, per month)

In order to help to determine my status to assess fees: I am affiliated with an educational institution (CSU), and this request is made for a scholarly or scientific purpose and not for commercial use. Thank you for your consideration of this request.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 3 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Jessie Harney

title: North Carolina DOC Prison-Month Level Data

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the North Carolina Public Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

This is a request under the Public Records Request. I would like to request both that a document containing facility-month level data from January 2024 through December 2025 be provided on the below measures for every facility managed or owned by North Carolina Department of Adult Correction. Please provide this information in a spreadsheet, if possible: 
* Overall staff budgeted positions (per prison, per month)
* Overall staff filled positions (per prison, per month)
* Custody staff budgeted positions (per prison, per month)
* Custody staff filled positions (per prison, per month)
* Average hours of overall staff overtime (per prison, per month)
* Average hours of custody staff overtime (per prison, per month)
* Average hours of overall staff sick leave (per prison, per month)
* Average hours of custody staff sick leave (per prison, per month)
* Overall population count (per prison, per month)
* Total number of infractions (per prison, per month)
* Total number of violent infractions (including the department’s definition of which infractions are defined as violent) (per prison, per month)
* Total number in restricted housing (per prison, per month)
* Total number of appeals from incarcerated individuals (per prison, per month)

I am also requesting the name and email of the current, acting, and/or incoming wardens for each facility managed or owned by North Carolina Department of Adult Correction. In order to help to determine my status to assess fees: I am affiliated with an educational institution (CSU), and this request is made for a scholarly or scientific purpose and not for commercial use. Thank you for your consideration of this request.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

Sincerely,

Jessie Harney

To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Damon Tabor
1201 W. 47th St.
Richmond, VA  23225
dreaded.media1@gmail.com

Defense Intelligence Agency
Attn: Freedom of Information Act/Declassification Review Office
RE: Mandatory Declassification Review (MDR) 


Dear DIA Freedom of Information Act and Declassification Review Officer:
Pursuant to Section 3.5 of Executive Order 13526 ("Classified National Security Information"), I respectfully submit this Mandatory Declassification Review (MDR) request for the following four (4) documents held by Library of Congress and identified below. I am a United States citizen and journalist with a lawful and substantial interest in the declassification and public release of the requested records.

1) "MISC PW MESSAGES/PWMIA DOCS/INSERTION TEAM TO INVESTIGATE SUSPECT PRISON COMPOUND NEAR NHO MORRAT WHICH MAY CONTAIN AMERICAN POW-MIAS" 
The document ID is PWMASTER_113969, dated March 17, 1981, currently held in the Library archive "Misc PW Files" (Reel 373, Pages 14-19).
2) "MUONG NHOMMARATH/LIVE SIGHTING OF AMERICAN POWS HELD IN DETENTION FACILITY SOUTHEAST OF NHOMMARATH, KHAMMOUAN PROVINCE, LAOS"
The document ID is PWMASTER_139390, dated August 31, 1980  (associated date: May 7, 1992), currently held in LoC archive "273" as part of the larger "Vietnam-Era Prisoner-Of-War/Missing-In-Action Database" (Reel 278, Pages 3-4).
3. "MISC PW MESSAGES/PWMIA DOCS/US POW/MIA"
The document ID is PWMASTER_114116, dated March 29, 1981 (associated date: April 28, 1981) currently held in LoC archive "373" as part of the larger "Vietnam-Era Prisoner-Of-War/Missing-In-Action Database" (Reel 373, Pages 62-68).
4) "MISC PW MESSAGES/PWMIA DOCS/ FORCES OPERATING IN LAOS"
The document ID is PWMASTER_114748, dated December 29, 1981 currently held in LoC archive ""373" as part of the larger "Vietnam-Era Prisoner-Of-War/Missing-In-Action Database" ( Reel 373, Pages 20-22).

I respectfully submit that the records at issue no longer satisfy the legal standards for continued classification, and that their continued withholding is inconsistent with the governing executive order, established declassification policy, and the public interest in governmental transparency. The following arguments support this request.

ARGUMENT I: THE PASSAGE OF TIME HAS RENDERED CONTINUED CLASSIFICATION UNJUSTIFIABLE
The document(s) at issue are of substantial age (45 years). The passage of time is among the most powerful and legally recognized grounds for declassification. Information that was legitimately sensitive at the time of its creation may — and frequently does — lose its sensitive character as the circumstances, technologies, relationships, and operational contexts that once gave rise to the classification concern are superseded by events.
This principle is not merely intuitive; it is codified in federal law and executive policy:
Executive Order 13526, § 3.3(a) mandates that agencies conduct systematic declassification reviews of records determined to have permanent historical value, and establishes a presumption of declassification for records twenty-five (25) or more years old. The records at issue fall squarely within this threshold.
The 25-Year Rule: Under § 3.3(b) of E.O. 13526, records that are more than 25 years old and have been determined to have permanent historical value are subject to automatic declassification unless they fall within a narrow set of enumerated exemptions. The DIA bears the burden of affirmatively identifying which specific exemption applies and articulating why it continues to apply with particularity.
Historical Obsolescence: The geopolitical landscape, technological environment, and intelligence relationships that existed at the time of this document's creation have fundamentally changed. Operations, alliances, and adversarial relationships referenced in records of this era have long since concluded, evolved, or become matters of extensive public record. Any sensitivity that may have once attached to this information has dissipated with the passage of time.
The DIA is respectfully urged to accord full weight to the passage of time in conducting this review.

ARGUMENT II: THE SOURCES AND METHODS REFERENCED IN THE DOCUMENT ARE OBSOLETE
One of the principal justifications for classifying intelligence records is the protection of sources and methods — the identities of human assets, technical collection platforms, signals intelligence capabilities, and clandestine relationships. However, this justification is only valid so long as the referenced sources and methods remain operationally active and their continued protection offers a genuine national security benefit. With respect to the document(s) at issue, there is substantial reason to conclude that:
Any human sources referenced in or discernible from the document are no longer active, and their identities — to the extent they could be inferred — are either deceased, no longer at risk, or already the subject of public reporting or historical scholarship. Any technical collection methods or platforms referenced are of a generation long since retired and replaced by successor systems. Disclosure of decades-old technical methods poses no risk to current intelligence operations and reveals nothing of value to adversaries that they could not have already determined through open-source means. Any foreign liaison relationships that may have been implicated by this document have either been publicly acknowledged, transformed beyond recognition, or dissolved. Where the sources and methods that originally justified classification are demonstrably obsolete, classification serves no protective function. Retention of classification under these circumstances is contrary to the letter and spirit of E.O. 13526 and constitutes the very type of classification that the Order's automatic declassification provisions were enacted to remedy.

ARGUMENT III: THE DIA CANNOT DEMONSTRATE "IDENTIFIABLE OR DESCRIBABLE DAMAGE" TO NATIONAL SECURITY
Classification authority under E.O. 13526 is not unlimited. Section 1.1(a) provides that information may only be classified if its "unauthorized disclosure could reasonably be expected to cause identifiable or describable damage to the national security." This is a demanding, concrete standard — not a generalized or precautionary one. A general assertion that a document is, or once was, classified is legally insufficient to sustain continued classification. The DIA must be able to:
-Identify the specific harm that disclosure would cause, with particularity;
-Describe why that harm would be "reasonably expected" to occur as a result of disclosure; and
-Demonstrate that the harm is cognizable under one of the eight enumerated classification categories set forth in § 1.4 of E.O. 13526.
-Given the age of the document(s) at issue and the transformation of the underlying factual circumstances, it is respectfully submitted that the DIA cannot meet this standard. Where the agency cannot articulate a specific and current harm, continued classification is not authorized and declassification is the appropriate result.

ARGUMENT IV: THE INFORMATION NO LONGER MEETS THE STANDARDS FOR CONTINUED CLASSIFICATION
The classification standards of E.O. 13526, § 1.1(a)(4), require that the "original classification authority" determine that "the unauthorized disclosure of the information reasonably could be expected to cause identifiable or describable damage to the national security." Critically, § 1.1(c) of the Order provides that "[i]n no case shall information be classified, continue to be maintained as classified, or fail to be declassified in order to: (1) conceal violations of law, inefficiency, or administrative error; (2) prevent embarrassment to a person, organization, or agency; (3) restrain competition; or (4) prevent or delay the release of information that does not require protection in the interest of the national security."
The document(s) at issue no longer satisfy the threshold requirements for classification under this standard. They do not, in 2026, protect the safety of citizens, the nation's democratic institutions, or ongoing intelligence operations. 

REQUESTED RELIEF
For the foregoing reasons, I respectfully request that the Defense Intelligence Agency:
1.   Conduct a full and thorough Mandatory Declassification Review of the document(s) identified above;
2.   Declassify and release the document(s) in full, or in the maximum degree possible with appropriate redactions of any information that continues to satisfy the classification standard with current particularity;
3.   Provide written notice of any portions withheld, including the specific exemption(s) invoked and the basis for each withholding, with sufficient specificity to permit a meaningful administrative appeal; and
4.   Process this request in accordance with the timelines prescribed by E.O. 13526 and applicable agency regulation.

Should any portion of this request be denied in whole or in part, I respectfully reserve my right to appeal the decision administratively pursuant to E.O. 13526, § 3.5(e), and thereafter to seek review before the Interagency Security Classification Appeals Panel (ISCAP) pursuant to § 5.3(b)(1) of the same Order. I appreciate the DIA's attention to this request and stand ready to provide any additional information that might facilitate processing. Please direct any correspondence regarding this matter to the address above.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Damon Tabor

title: New Hampshire DOC Request (prison-month level data)

communications.subject: New Hampshire Right to Know Law Request: New Hampshire DOC Request (prison-month level data)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the New Hampshire Right to Know Law, I hereby request the following records:

This is a request under the Right to Know Request. I would like to request a document containing facility-month level data from January 2024 through December 2025 be provided on the below measures for every facility managed or owned by New Hampshire Department of Corrections. 
• Overall staff budgeted positions (per prison, per month)
• Overall staff filled positions  (per prison, per month)
• Custody staff budgeted positions  (per prison, per month)
• Custody staff filled positions  (per prison, per month)
• Average hours of overall staff overtime used (per prison, per month)
• Average hours of custody staff overtime used (per prison, per month)
• Average hours of overall staff sick leave used (per prison, per month)
• Average hours of custody staff sick leave used (per prison, per month)
• Overall population count  (per prison, per month)
• Total number of infractions  (per prison, per month)
• Total number of violent infractions (including the department’s definition of which infractions are defined as violent)  (per prison, per month)
• Total number in restricted housing (per prison, per month)
• Total number of appeals (grievances) from incarcerated individuals (per prison, per month)

I am also requesting the name and email of the current, acting, and/or incoming wardens for each facility. This request is made for a scholarly or scientific purpose and not for commercial use. Thank you for your consideration of this request.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Jessie Harney
